source.title: Differential effects of social and novelty enrichment on individual differences in impulsivity and behavioral flexibility

confidence_rationale: Behavioural Brain Research; systematically separates social vs. novelty enrichment components and their effects on impulsivity and behavioral flexibility (reversal learning). Finding is mixed: social enrichment decreased impulsive choice but increased regressive errors; novelty enrichment increased impulsive choice; both reduced never-reinforced errors. The nuanced dissociation is informative but also complicates a simple 'more enrichment = more flexibility' narrative.
failure_signatures: Social enrichment increased regressive errors in behavioral flexibility (more perseveration on previously-rewarded options), which is the opposite of the expected result if social enrichment boosts hippocampal option diversity.
The specific direction of the flexibility effect depends on the enrichment type -- a simple 'more exploration = better flexibility' account is not supported.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Social and novelty enrichment produce distinct and partially opposing effects on behavioral flexibility and impulsivity; novelty enrichment reduces never-reinforced errors (reduces inflexibility about un-tried options) while social enrichment increases regressive errors (increases perseveration on previously-rewarded options).
mapping.ree_translation: The dissociation between novelty enrichment (reduces inflexibility about untried options -- expands option representation) and social enrichment (increases perseveration -- narrows behavioral control) is an important caveat. The claim that 'sufficient early exploration bootstraps behavioral diversity' needs qualification: it is exploratory variety (novelty), not mere social complexity, that most directly expands the behavioral option store.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The task used (multiple choice operant) differs substantially from spatial navigation; the mapping from 'novelty enrichment reduces never-reinforced errors' to 'hippocampal trajectory store is more populated' is inferential.
mapping.source_context: Sprague-Dawley rats; four rearing conditions (isolated/social x no-novelty/novelty); operant impulsivity, behavioral flexibility, and impulsive action tasks.

confidence_components.notes: Nuanced result; the dissociation complicates but does not refute the developmental bootstrapping claim; novelty > social for option-space expansion.
